items.passage: A catalyst is a substance that speeds up a chemical reaction without being used up in the process. It works by lowering the energy that molecules need before they can react with each other. Many industrial factories rely on metal catalysts to produce fuels, plastics, and fertilizers at lower cost. Inside living cells, special proteins called enzymes act as natural catalysts for digestion. Without these helpful substances, countless reactions would happen far too slowly to support modern life.

Three-dimensional printing builds solid objects one thin layer at a time from a digital design. A machine reads the plan and deposits melted plastic, metal, or resin in precise shapes. Engineers use this method to create lightweight parts that traditional tools cannot easily produce. Doctors have even printed custom models of bones to prepare for difficult surgeries. Because the process wastes very little material, many companies view it as a cleaner way to manufacture goods.

Fresco is an ancient painting technique in which colors are applied directly onto fresh, wet plaster. As the plaster dries, the pigments become a permanent part of the wall itself. Renaissance artists favored this durable method for decorating the ceilings of grand churches and palaces. The painter had to work quickly before each section of plaster hardened beyond use. Many frescoes have survived for centuries, allowing modern viewers to admire scenes from a distant age.

Urbanization describes the steady movement of people from rural villages toward crowded cities. Families often relocate in search of better jobs, schools, and access to medical care. Rapid growth can strain the housing, transport, and water systems that a city already provides. Planners therefore design new neighborhoods to balance population density against open public space. When managed wisely, expanding cities can spread wealth and ideas across an entire region.

Ocean tides are the regular rise and fall of sea levels along the world's coastlines. They are caused mainly by the gravitational pull of the Moon upon the Earth's vast oceans. As the planet rotates, each shoreline passes through periods of high and low water twice daily. The Sun also tugs on the seas, strengthening tides when it lines up with the Moon. Coastal communities have long studied these movements to plan fishing trips and safe harbors.

International trade allows nations to exchange goods and services across distant borders. Each country tends to export products that it can make cheaply and import those it lacks. This exchange gives shoppers a wider choice of foods, clothes, and gadgets than any single nation produces. Governments sometimes add taxes called tariffs to protect their own factories from foreign competition. Despite such barriers, global trade has connected markets and lifted living standards in many places.
